---
title: "ML Methods: Salary Prediction"
format:
  html:
    theme: cerulean
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    embed-resources: true
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
date-modified: today
date-format: long
execute:
  echo: true
  eval: true
  freeze: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Use Multiple Linear Regression to model salary based on location, job title, skills, remote type, employment type, minimum experience, and education level.

### Load data
# Multiple Linear Regression Salary Analysis

This section uses Multiple Linear Regression to predict salary from job posting characteristics. We first build a baseline model, then use feature ablation to identify which feature groups matter most. Based on the ablation results, we build an improved Model 2 and compare the two models.

## Model 1: Baseline Multiple Linear Regression

Model 1 predicts salary using seven main feature groups: location, job title, skills, remote work type, employment type, minimum years of experience, and education level.

```{python}

#| echo: false

#| warning: false

#| message: false

from pyspark.sql import SparkSession

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, when, trim, regexp_replace, count as spark_count, lower

from pyspark.ml.feature import StringIndexer, OneHotEncoder, VectorAssembler

from pyspark.ml.regression import LinearRegression

from pyspark.ml import Pipeline

from pyspark.ml.evaluation import RegressionEvaluator

from IPython.display import display

import pandas as pd

import plotly.express as px

spark = (
    SparkSession.builder
    .config("spark.driver.host", "localhost")
    .config("spark.driver.memory", "4g")
    .config("spark.executor.memory", "4g")
    .appName("SalaryMLR")
    .getOrCreate()
)

spark.catalog.clearCache()

spark.sparkContext.setLogLevel("ERROR")

# Load prepared model-ready dataset from data_cleaning.qmd

df_model1_base = spark.read.parquet("data/prepared/salary_regression_data.parquet")

skill_count_features_df = spark.read.parquet("data/prepared/skill_count_features.parquet")

df_model1_base = df_model1_base.join(

    skill_count_features_df,

    df_model1_base.id == skill_count_features_df.job_id,

    how="left"

).drop("job_id")

# Add education level from cleaned dataset

education_df_model1 = (

    spark.read.parquet("data/cleaned/lightcast_cleaned.parquet")

    .select(

        col("ID").alias("id"),

        col("EDUCATION_LEVELS_NAME").alias("education_level")

    )

    .dropDuplicates(["id"])

)

df_model1_base = (

    df_model1_base

    .join(education_df_model1, on="id", how="left")

    .withColumn(

        "education_level",

        trim(

            regexp_replace(

                regexp_replace(col("education_level"), r"[\n\r]", " "),

                r"\s+",

                " "

            )

        )

    )

    .fillna({"education_level": "Unknown"})

)

# Keep only valid salary rows

df_salary1 = df_model1_base.filter(

    col("salary").isNotNull() &

    (col("salary") > 0) &

    (col("salary") <= 500000)

).cache()


# Group job titles using top 30 titles

top_titles_model1 = [

    r["title_clean"] for r in

    df_salary1.groupBy("title_clean")

    .agg(spark_count("*").alias("cnt"))

    .orderBy(col("cnt").desc())

    .limit(30)

    .collect()

]

df_model1 = df_salary1.withColumn(

    "title_grouped",

    when(col("title_clean").isin(top_titles_model1), col("title_clean")).otherwise("Other")

)

# Skill features prepared in data_cleaning.qmd

skill_cols_model1 = [

    "has_python",

    "has_sql",

    "has_machine_learning",

    "has_tableau",

    "has_power_bi",

    "has_aws",

    "has_excel",

    "has_sap",

    "common_skill_count",

    "specialized_skill_count",

    "software_skill_count"

]

# Select Model 1 features

df_model1 = (

    df_model1

    .select(

        ["salary",

         "state_name",

         "title_grouped",

         "remote_type_name",

         "employment_type_name",

         "min_years_experience",

         "education_level"] + skill_cols_model1

    )

    .fillna({

        "state_name": "Unknown",

        "title_grouped": "Other",

        "remote_type_name": "Unknown",

        "employment_type_name": "Unknown",

        "min_years_experience": 0,

        "education_level": "Unknown",

        "has_python": 0,

        "has_sql": 0,

        "has_machine_learning": 0,

        "has_tableau": 0,

        "has_power_bi": 0,

        "has_aws": 0,

        "has_excel": 0,

        "has_sap": 0,

        "common_skill_count": 0,

        "specialized_skill_count": 0,

        "software_skill_count": 0

    })

    .cache()

)

print("Model 1 rows:", df_model1.count())

print("Model 1 top 30 grouped titles:", top_titles_model1[:10], "...")

# Model 1 categorical encoders

title_indexer_model1 = StringIndexer(

    inputCol="title_grouped",

    outputCol="title_idx",

    handleInvalid="keep"

)

title_encoder_model1 = OneHotEncoder(

    inputCol="title_idx",

    outputCol="title_vec",

    dropLast=False

)

state_indexer_model1 = StringIndexer(

    inputCol="state_name",

    outputCol="state_idx",

    handleInvalid="keep"

)

state_encoder_model1 = OneHotEncoder(

    inputCol="state_idx",

    outputCol="state_vec",

    dropLast=False

)

remote_indexer_model1 = StringIndexer(

    inputCol="remote_type_name",

    outputCol="remote_idx",

    handleInvalid="keep"

)

remote_encoder_model1 = OneHotEncoder(

    inputCol="remote_idx",

    outputCol="remote_vec",

    dropLast=False

)

employment_indexer_model1 = StringIndexer(

    inputCol="employment_type_name",

    outputCol="employment_idx",

    handleInvalid="keep"

)

employment_encoder_model1 = OneHotEncoder(

    inputCol="employment_idx",

    outputCol="employment_vec",

    dropLast=False

)

education_indexer_model1 = StringIndexer(

    inputCol="education_level",

    outputCol="education_idx",

    handleInvalid="keep"

)

education_encoder_model1 = OneHotEncoder(

    inputCol="education_idx",

    outputCol="education_vec",

    dropLast=False

)

assembler_model1 = VectorAssembler(

    inputCols=[

        "title_vec",

        "state_vec",

        "remote_vec",

        "employment_vec",

        "education_vec",

        "min_years_experience"

    ] + skill_cols_model1,

    outputCol="features"

)

lr_model1_estimator = LinearRegression(

    featuresCol="features",

    labelCol="salary"

)

pipeline_model1 = Pipeline(stages=[

    title_indexer_model1, title_encoder_model1,

    state_indexer_model1, state_encoder_model1,

    remote_indexer_model1, remote_encoder_model1,

    employment_indexer_model1, employment_encoder_model1,

    education_indexer_model1, education_encoder_model1,

    assembler_model1, lr_model1_estimator

])

train_model1, test_model1 = df_model1.randomSplit([0.8, 0.2], seed=42)

model1 = pipeline_model1.fit(train_model1)

predictions_model1 = model1.transform(test_model1)

# Model 1 metrics

r2_model1 = RegressionEvaluator(

    labelCol="salary",

    predictionCol="prediction",

    metricName="r2"

).evaluate(predictions_model1)

rmse_model1 = RegressionEvaluator(

    labelCol="salary",

    predictionCol="prediction",

    metricName="rmse"

).evaluate(predictions_model1)

mae_model1 = RegressionEvaluator(

    labelCol="salary",

    predictionCol="prediction",

    metricName="mae"

).evaluate(predictions_model1)

lr_model1 = model1.stages[-1]

n_model1 = predictions_model1.count()

p_model1 = len(lr_model1.coefficients)

adjusted_r2_model1 = (

    1 - ((1 - r2_model1) * (n_model1 - 1) / (n_model1 - p_model1 - 1))

    if n_model1 > p_model1 + 1

    else None

)

metrics_df_model1 = pd.DataFrame({

    "Metric": ["R²", "Adjusted R²", "RMSE", "MAE"],

    "Value": [

        round(r2_model1, 4),

        round(adjusted_r2_model1, 4) if adjusted_r2_model1 is not None else None,

        round(rmse_model1, 2),

        round(mae_model1, 2)

    ]

})

display(metrics_df_model1)

# Salary benchmark for interpreting RMSE/MAE scale
salary_stats_model1 = df_salary1.selectExpr(
    "min(salary) as min_salary",
    "percentile_approx(salary, 0.5) as median_salary",
    "max(salary) as max_salary"
).collect()[0]

salary_benchmark_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Statistic": ["Min Salary", "Median Salary", "Max Salary"],
    "Value": [
        round(salary_stats_model1["min_salary"], 2),
        round(salary_stats_model1["median_salary"], 2),
        round(salary_stats_model1["max_salary"], 2)
    ]
})

print("\n=== Salary Distribution Benchmark (for interpreting RMSE/MAE) ===")
display(salary_benchmark_df)

rmse_pct_of_median_model1 = rmse_model1 / salary_stats_model1["median_salary"] * 100
mae_pct_of_median_model1 = mae_model1 / salary_stats_model1["median_salary"] * 100

print(f"\nModel 1 RMSE is {rmse_pct_of_median_model1:.1f}% of median salary")
print(f"Model 1 MAE is {mae_pct_of_median_model1:.1f}% of median salary")

```

To judge whether an RMSE or MAE value represents a large or small error, we express it as a percentage of the median salary rather than reading the raw dollar amount in isolation.

For example, Model 1 RMSE being 32.1% of median salary means the model's typical prediction error ($36,527) is equivalent to about a third of the typical salary in our dataset ($113,800) -- a meaningful, non-trivial error.

Since this gap is fairly large, we proceed to refine the model in Model 2, adding features and structure to try to close it.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

# Model 1 coefficient table

coefficients_model1 = lr_model1.coefficients.toArray()

title_labels_model1 = list(model1.stages[0].labels) + ["title_unseen"]

state_labels_model1 = list(model1.stages[2].labels) + ["state_unseen"]

remote_labels_model1 = list(model1.stages[4].labels) + ["remote_unseen"]

employment_labels_model1 = list(model1.stages[6].labels) + ["employment_unseen"]

education_labels_model1 = list(model1.stages[8].labels) + ["education_unseen"]

feature_names_model1 = (

    ["Title: " + x for x in title_labels_model1] +

    ["State: " + x for x in state_labels_model1] +

    ["Remote: " + x for x in remote_labels_model1] +

    ["Employment: " + x for x in employment_labels_model1] +

    ["Education: " + x for x in education_labels_model1] +

    ["Minimum Years Experience"] +

    ["Skill: " + x.replace("has_", "").replace("_", " ").title() for x in skill_cols_model1]

)

feature_names_model1 = feature_names_model1[:len(coefficients_model1)]

coef_df_model1 = pd.DataFrame({

    "Feature": feature_names_model1,

    "Coefficient": coefficients_model1

})

coef_df_model1 = coef_df_model1[

    ~coef_df_model1["Feature"].str.contains("unseen", case=False, na=False)

].sort_values("Coefficient", ascending=False)
```


### Feature Coefficients

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

print("\n=== Model 1: Salary Impact by Feature ===")

display(coef_df_model1)

print(f"\nModel 1 Intercept: ${lr_model1.intercept:,.0f}")

print(f"Model 1 Test R²: {r2_model1:.4f}")

print(

    f"Model 1 Test Adjusted R²: {adjusted_r2_model1:.4f}"

    if adjusted_r2_model1 is not None

    else "Model 1 Adjusted R²: not available"

)

```

## Which Features Impact Salary? (Model 1)

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

coef_plot_df_model1 = coef_df_model1.copy()
coef_plot_df_model1["abs_coef"] = coef_plot_df_model1["Coefficient"].abs()

top_coef_plot_model1 = (
    coef_plot_df_model1
    .sort_values("abs_coef", ascending=False)
    .head(20)
    .sort_values("Coefficient")
    .copy()
)

top_coef_plot_model1["direction"] = top_coef_plot_model1["Coefficient"].apply(
    lambda x: "Positive salary association" if x > 0 else "Negative salary association"
)

top_coef_plot_model1["label"] = top_coef_plot_model1["Coefficient"].apply(
    lambda x: f"${x:,.0f}"
)

def shorten_feature_name_model1(x):
    if x.startswith("Education: "):
        x = x.replace("Education: ", "Edu: ")
        x = x.replace("High school or GED", "HS/GED")
        x = x.replace("Associate degree", "Assoc.")
        x = x.replace("Bachelor's degree", "Bachelor")
        x = x.replace("Master's degree", "Master")
        x = x.replace("Ph.D. or professional degree", "PhD")
        x = x.replace(", ", " + ")

    if x.startswith("Title: "):
        title = x.replace("Title: ", "")
        if len(title) > 42:
            title = title[:42] + "..."
        x = "Title: " + title

    if len(x) > 70:
        x = x[:70] + "..."

    return x

top_coef_plot_model1["Feature_short"] = top_coef_plot_model1["Feature"].apply(shorten_feature_name_model1)

top_coef_plot_model1["Feature_display"] = top_coef_plot_model1.apply(
    lambda r: f"{r['Feature_short']}  ({r['label']})",
    axis=1
)

fig_coef_model1 = px.bar(
    top_coef_plot_model1,
    x="Coefficient",
    y="Feature_display",
    color="direction",
    orientation="h",
    title="Top Salary Predictors by Absolute Coefficient (Model 1)",
    labels={
        "Coefficient": "Estimated Salary Impact ($)",
        "Feature_display": ""
    },
    color_discrete_map={
        "Positive salary association": "#2ca02c",
        "Negative salary association": "#d62728"
    },
    hover_data={
        "Feature": True,
        "Coefficient": ":,.0f",
        "direction": False,
        "Feature_short": False,
        "Feature_display": False,
        "label": False,
        "abs_coef": False
    }
)

fig_coef_model1.update_layout(
    template="plotly_white",
    height=900,
    width=1150,
    margin=dict(l=520, r=80, t=90, b=70),
    showlegend=True,
    legend=dict(
        orientation="h",
        yanchor="bottom",
        y=1.03,
        xanchor="right",
        x=1
    ),
    title=dict(x=0.02, xanchor="left"),
    xaxis=dict(
        title="Estimated Salary Impact ($)",
        tickprefix="$",
        separatethousands=True,
        gridcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0.08)"
    ),
    yaxis=dict(
        title="",
        automargin=True
    )
)

fig_coef_model1.show()
```

## Model 1 Feature Ablation

The ablation test removes one feature group at a time to see how much model performance decreases. A larger R² drop or larger RMSE/MAE increase means the removed feature group is more important.

```{python}

#| echo: false

#| warning: false

#| message: false

base_feature_groups_model1 = {

    "Job Title": ["title_vec"],

    "Location": ["state_vec"],

    "Remote Type": ["remote_vec"],

    "Employment Type": ["employment_vec"],

    "Education": ["education_vec"],

    "Experience": ["min_years_experience"],

    "Skills": skill_cols_model1

}

base_transform_stages_model1 = [

    title_indexer_model1, title_encoder_model1,

    state_indexer_model1, state_encoder_model1,

    remote_indexer_model1, remote_encoder_model1,

    employment_indexer_model1, employment_encoder_model1,

    education_indexer_model1, education_encoder_model1

]

def evaluate_without_group_model1(group_to_remove):

    selected_inputs = []

    for group_name, cols in base_feature_groups_model1.items():

        if group_name != group_to_remove:

            selected_inputs.extend(cols)

    temp_assembler = VectorAssembler(

        inputCols=selected_inputs,

        outputCol="features"

    )

    temp_lr = LinearRegression(

        featuresCol="features",

        labelCol="salary"

    )

    temp_pipeline = Pipeline(

        stages=base_transform_stages_model1 + [temp_assembler, temp_lr]

    )

    temp_model = temp_pipeline.fit(train_model1)

    temp_predictions = temp_model.transform(test_model1)

    temp_r2 = RegressionEvaluator(

        labelCol="salary",

        predictionCol="prediction",

        metricName="r2"

    ).evaluate(temp_predictions)

    temp_rmse = RegressionEvaluator(

        labelCol="salary",

        predictionCol="prediction",

        metricName="rmse"

    ).evaluate(temp_predictions)

    temp_mae = RegressionEvaluator(

        labelCol="salary",

        predictionCol="prediction",

        metricName="mae"

    ).evaluate(temp_predictions)

    return temp_r2, temp_rmse, temp_mae

ablation_results_model1 = []

for group_name in base_feature_groups_model1.keys():

    temp_r2, temp_rmse, temp_mae = evaluate_without_group_model1(group_name)

    ablation_results_model1.append({

        "Removed Feature Group": group_name,

        "R² Without Group": round(temp_r2, 4),

        "R² Drop": round(r2_model1 - temp_r2, 4),

        "RMSE Without Group": round(temp_rmse, 2),

        "RMSE Increase": round(temp_rmse - rmse_model1, 2),

        "MAE Without Group": round(temp_mae, 2),

        "MAE Increase": round(temp_mae - mae_model1, 2)

    })

ablation_df_model1 = pd.DataFrame(ablation_results_model1).sort_values("R² Drop", ascending=False)

display(ablation_df_model1)

```

The ablation results guide the design of Model 2. Feature groups with larger R² drops are kept or improved, while weak feature groups are removed or treated as lower priority.

## Model 2: Improved Multiple Linear Regression

Model 2 improves the baseline model by adding occupation group and specialized occupation, using cleaned education categories, keeping the top 30 grouped job titles to limit overfitting, and focusing on stronger predictors. Employment type and AI-related status are removed from Model 2 because they showed very small contribution in the ablation results.

```{python}

#| echo: false

#| warning: false

#| message: false

# Load prepared model-ready dataset from data_cleaning.qmd

df_model2_base = spark.read.parquet("data/prepared/salary_regression_data.parquet")

df_model2_base = df_model2_base.join(

    skill_count_features_df,

    df_model2_base.id == skill_count_features_df.job_id,

    how="left"

).drop("job_id")

# Add education level from cleaned dataset

education_df_model2 = (

    spark.read.parquet("data/cleaned/lightcast_cleaned.parquet")

    .select(

        col("ID").alias("id"),

        col("EDUCATION_LEVELS_NAME").alias("education_level")

    )

    .dropDuplicates(["id"])

)

df_model2_base = (

    df_model2_base

    .join(education_df_model2, on="id", how="left")

    .withColumn(

        "education_level",

        trim(

            regexp_replace(

                regexp_replace(col("education_level"), r"[\n\r]", " "),

                r"\s+",

                " "

            )

        )

    )

    .fillna({"education_level": "Unknown"})

)

# Clean education into simpler categories

df_model2_base = df_model2_base.withColumn(

    "education_level_clean",

    when(lower(col("education_level")).contains("ph.d"), "PhD / Professional")

    .when(lower(col("education_level")).contains("professional degree"), "PhD / Professional")

    .when(lower(col("education_level")).contains("master"), "Master")

    .when(lower(col("education_level")).contains("bachelor"), "Bachelor")

    .when(lower(col("education_level")).contains("associate"), "Associate")

    .when(lower(col("education_level")).contains("high school"), "High School / GED")

    .otherwise("Unknown")

)

# Keep only valid salary rows

df_salary2 = df_model2_base.filter(

    col("salary").isNotNull() &

    (col("salary") > 0) &

    (col("salary") <= 500000)

).cache()

# Keep top 30 job titles to avoid too many sparse dummy variables

top_titles_model2 = [

    r["title_clean"] for r in

    df_salary2.groupBy("title_clean")

    .agg(spark_count("*").alias("cnt"))

    .orderBy(col("cnt").desc())

    .limit(30)

    .collect()

]

df_model2 = df_salary2.withColumn(

    "title_grouped",

    when(col("title_clean").isin(top_titles_model2), col("title_clean")).otherwise("Other")

)

# Focused skill features

skill_cols_model2 = [

    "has_python",

    "has_sql",

    "has_machine_learning",

    "has_tableau",

    "has_power_bi",

    "has_aws",

    "has_excel",

    "has_sap",

    "specialized_skill_count",

    "software_skill_count"

]

# Select Model 2 features

# Select Model 2 features
df_model2 = (
    df_model2
    .select(
        ["salary",
         "state_name",
         "title_grouped",
         "occupation_group_name",
         "specialized_occupation_name",
         "remote_type_name",
         "employment_type_name",
         "min_years_experience",
         "education_level_clean",
         "is_ai_related"] + skill_cols_model2
    )
    .fillna({
        "state_name": "Unknown",
        "title_grouped": "Other",
        "occupation_group_name": "Unknown",
        "specialized_occupation_name": "Unknown",
        "remote_type_name": "Unknown",
        "employment_type_name": "Unknown",
        "min_years_experience": 0,
        "education_level_clean": "Unknown",
        "is_ai_related": 0,
        "has_python": 0,
        "has_sql": 0,
        "has_machine_learning": 0,
        "has_tableau": 0,
        "has_power_bi": 0,
        "has_aws": 0,
        "has_excel": 0,
        "has_sap": 0,
        "specialized_skill_count": 0,
        "software_skill_count": 0
    })
    .cache()
)

print("Model 2 rows:", df_model2.count())

print("Model 2 top 30 grouped titles:", top_titles_model2[:10], "...")

# Model 2 categorical encoders

title_indexer_model2 = StringIndexer(

    inputCol="title_grouped",

    outputCol="title_idx",

    handleInvalid="keep"

)

title_encoder_model2 = OneHotEncoder(

    inputCol="title_idx",

    outputCol="title_vec",

    dropLast=False

)

occupation_group_indexer_model2 = StringIndexer(

    inputCol="occupation_group_name",

    outputCol="occupation_group_idx",

    handleInvalid="keep"

)

occupation_group_encoder_model2 = OneHotEncoder(

    inputCol="occupation_group_idx",

    outputCol="occupation_group_vec",

    dropLast=False

)

specialized_occupation_indexer_model2 = StringIndexer(

    inputCol="specialized_occupation_name",

    outputCol="specialized_occupation_idx",

    handleInvalid="keep"

)

specialized_occupation_encoder_model2 = OneHotEncoder(

    inputCol="specialized_occupation_idx",

    outputCol="specialized_occupation_vec",

    dropLast=False

)

state_indexer_model2 = StringIndexer(

    inputCol="state_name",

    outputCol="state_idx",

    handleInvalid="keep"

)

state_encoder_model2 = OneHotEncoder(

    inputCol="state_idx",

    outputCol="state_vec",

    dropLast=False

)

remote_indexer_model2 = StringIndexer(

    inputCol="remote_type_name",

    outputCol="remote_idx",

    handleInvalid="keep"

)

remote_encoder_model2 = OneHotEncoder(

    inputCol="remote_idx",

    outputCol="remote_vec",

    dropLast=False

)
employment_indexer_model2 = StringIndexer(
    inputCol="employment_type_name",
    outputCol="employment_idx",
    handleInvalid="keep"
)

employment_encoder_model2 = OneHotEncoder(
    inputCol="employment_idx",
    outputCol="employment_vec",
    dropLast=False
)

education_indexer_model2 = StringIndexer(

    inputCol="education_level_clean",

    outputCol="education_idx",

    handleInvalid="keep"

)

education_encoder_model2 = OneHotEncoder(

    inputCol="education_idx",

    outputCol="education_vec",

    dropLast=False

)

assembler_model2 = VectorAssembler(
    inputCols=[
        "title_vec",
        "occupation_group_vec",
        "specialized_occupation_vec",
        "state_vec",
        "remote_vec",
        "employment_vec",
        "education_vec",
        "min_years_experience",
        "is_ai_related"
    ] + skill_cols_model2,
    outputCol="features"
)

lr_model2_estimator = LinearRegression(

    featuresCol="features",

    labelCol="salary"

)

pipeline_model2 = Pipeline(stages=[
    title_indexer_model2, title_encoder_model2,
    occupation_group_indexer_model2, occupation_group_encoder_model2,
    specialized_occupation_indexer_model2, specialized_occupation_encoder_model2,
    state_indexer_model2, state_encoder_model2,
    remote_indexer_model2, remote_encoder_model2,
    employment_indexer_model2, employment_encoder_model2,
    education_indexer_model2, education_encoder_model2,
    assembler_model2, lr_model2_estimator
])

train_model2, test_model2 = df_model2.randomSplit([0.8, 0.2], seed=42)

model2 = pipeline_model2.fit(train_model2)

predictions_model2 = model2.transform(test_model2)

# Model 2 metrics

r2_model2 = RegressionEvaluator(

    labelCol="salary",

    predictionCol="prediction",

    metricName="r2"

).evaluate(predictions_model2)

rmse_model2 = RegressionEvaluator(

    labelCol="salary",

    predictionCol="prediction",

    metricName="rmse"

).evaluate(predictions_model2)

mae_model2 = RegressionEvaluator(

    labelCol="salary",

    predictionCol="prediction",

    metricName="mae"

).evaluate(predictions_model2)

lr_model2 = model2.stages[-1]

n_model2 = predictions_model2.count()

p_model2 = len(lr_model2.coefficients)

adjusted_r2_model2 = (

    1 - ((1 - r2_model2) * (n_model2 - 1) / (n_model2 - p_model2 - 1))

    if n_model2 > p_model2 + 1

    else None

)

metrics_df_model2 = pd.DataFrame({

    "Metric": ["R²", "Adjusted R²", "RMSE", "MAE"],

    "Value": [

        round(r2_model2, 4),

        round(adjusted_r2_model2, 4) if adjusted_r2_model2 is not None else None,

        round(rmse_model2, 2),

        round(mae_model2, 2)

    ]

})

display(metrics_df_model2)

rmse_pct_of_median_model2 = rmse_model2 / salary_stats_model1["median_salary"] * 100
mae_pct_of_median_model2 = mae_model2 / salary_stats_model1["median_salary"] * 100
print(f"Model 2 RMSE is {rmse_pct_of_median_model2:.1f}% of median salary")
print(f"Model 2 MAE is {mae_pct_of_median_model2:.1f}% of median salary")

# Model 2 coefficient table
coefficients_model2 = lr_model2.coefficients.toArray()

title_labels_model2 = list(model2.stages[0].labels) + ["title_unseen"]
occupation_group_labels_model2 = list(model2.stages[2].labels) + ["occupation_group_unseen"]
specialized_occupation_labels_model2 = list(model2.stages[4].labels) + ["specialized_occupation_unseen"]
state_labels_model2 = list(model2.stages[6].labels) + ["state_unseen"]
remote_labels_model2 = list(model2.stages[8].labels) + ["remote_unseen"]
employment_labels_model2 = list(model2.stages[10].labels) + ["employment_unseen"]
education_labels_model2 = list(model2.stages[12].labels) + ["education_unseen"]

feature_names_model2 = (
    ["Title: " + x for x in title_labels_model2] +
    ["Occupation Group: " + x for x in occupation_group_labels_model2] +
    ["Specialized Occupation: " + x for x in specialized_occupation_labels_model2] +
    ["State: " + x for x in state_labels_model2] +
    ["Remote: " + x for x in remote_labels_model2] +
    ["Employment: " + x for x in employment_labels_model2] +
    ["Education: " + x for x in education_labels_model2] +
    ["Minimum Years Experience"] +
    ["Is AI Related"] +
    ["Skill: " + x.replace("has_", "").replace("_", " ").title() for x in skill_cols_model2]
)

feature_names_model2 = feature_names_model2[:len(coefficients_model2)]

coef_df_model2 = pd.DataFrame({

    "Feature": feature_names_model2,

    "Coefficient": coefficients_model2

})

coef_df_model2 = coef_df_model2[

    ~coef_df_model2["Feature"].str.contains("unseen", case=False, na=False)

].sort_values("Coefficient", ascending=False)

```


### Feature Coefficients

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

print("\n=== Model 2: Salary Impact by Feature ===")

display(coef_df_model2)

print(f"\nModel 2 Intercept: ${lr_model2.intercept:,.0f}")

print(f"Model 2 Test R²: {r2_model2:.4f}")

print(

    f"Model 2 Test Adjusted R²: {adjusted_r2_model2:.4f}"

    if adjusted_r2_model2 is not None

    else "Model 2 Adjusted R²: not available"

)

```

## Which Features Impact Salary? (Model 2)

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

coef_plot_df_model2 = coef_df_model2.copy()
coef_plot_df_model2["abs_coef"] = coef_plot_df_model2["Coefficient"].abs()

top_coef_plot_model2 = (
    coef_plot_df_model2
    .sort_values("abs_coef", ascending=False)
    .head(20)
    .sort_values("Coefficient")
    .copy()
)

top_coef_plot_model2["direction"] = top_coef_plot_model2["Coefficient"].apply(
    lambda x: "Positive salary association" if x > 0 else "Negative salary association"
)

top_coef_plot_model2["label"] = top_coef_plot_model2["Coefficient"].apply(
    lambda x: f"${x:,.0f}"
)

def shorten_feature_name_model2(x):
    if x.startswith("Education: "):
        x = x.replace("Education: ", "Edu: ")
        x = x.replace("High school or GED", "HS/GED")
        x = x.replace("Associate degree", "Assoc.")
        x = x.replace("Bachelor's degree", "Bachelor")
        x = x.replace("Master's degree", "Master")
        x = x.replace("Ph.D. or professional degree", "PhD")
        x = x.replace(", ", " + ")

    if x.startswith("Title: "):
        title = x.replace("Title: ", "")
        if len(title) > 42:
            title = title[:42] + "..."
        x = "Title: " + title

    if len(x) > 70:
        x = x[:70] + "..."

    return x

top_coef_plot_model2["Feature_short"] = top_coef_plot_model2["Feature"].apply(shorten_feature_name_model2)

top_coef_plot_model2["Feature_display"] = top_coef_plot_model2.apply(
    lambda r: f"{r['Feature_short']}  ({r['label']})",
    axis=1
)

fig_coef_model2 = px.bar(
    top_coef_plot_model2,
    x="Coefficient",
    y="Feature_display",
    color="direction",
    orientation="h",
    title="Top Salary Predictors by Absolute Coefficient (Model 2)",
    labels={
        "Coefficient": "Estimated Salary Impact ($)",
        "Feature_display": ""
    },
    color_discrete_map={
        "Positive salary association": "#2ca02c",
        "Negative salary association": "#d62728"
    },
    hover_data={
        "Feature": True,
        "Coefficient": ":,.0f",
        "direction": False,
        "Feature_short": False,
        "Feature_display": False,
        "label": False,
        "abs_coef": False
    }
)

fig_coef_model2.update_layout(
    template="plotly_white",
    height=900,
    width=1150,
    margin=dict(l=520, r=80, t=90, b=70),
    showlegend=True,
    legend=dict(
        orientation="h",
        yanchor="bottom",
        y=1.03,
        xanchor="right",
        x=1
    ),
    title=dict(x=0.02, xanchor="left"),
    xaxis=dict(
        title="Estimated Salary Impact ($)",
        tickprefix="$",
        separatethousands=True,
        gridcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0.08)"
    ),
    yaxis=dict(
        title="",
        automargin=True
    )
)

fig_coef_model2.show()
```

### Try different job features and skills to see how each feature changes predicted salary.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| results: asis

import json
from IPython.display import HTML

# Collect option lists from Model 2
state_options = sorted([
    x for x in df_model2.select("state_name").distinct().toPandas()["state_name"].dropna().tolist()
])

title_options = sorted([
    x for x in df_model2.select("title_grouped").distinct().toPandas()["title_grouped"].dropna().tolist()
])

occupation_group_options = sorted([
    x for x in df_model2.select("occupation_group_name").distinct().toPandas()["occupation_group_name"].dropna().tolist()
])

specialized_occupation_options = sorted([
    x for x in df_model2.select("specialized_occupation_name").distinct().toPandas()["specialized_occupation_name"].dropna().tolist()
])

remote_options = sorted([
    x for x in df_model2.select("remote_type_name").distinct().toPandas()["remote_type_name"].dropna().tolist()
])

education_options = sorted([
    x for x in df_model2.select("education_level_clean").distinct().toPandas()["education_level_clean"].dropna().tolist()
])

employment_options = sorted([
    x for x in df_model2.select("employment_type_name").distinct().toPandas()["employment_type_name"].dropna().tolist()
])
# Create coefficient lookup from Model 2
coef_lookup_model2 = dict(zip(feature_names_model2, coefficients_model2))

payload = {
    "intercept": float(lr_model2.intercept),
    "coef": {k: float(v) for k, v in coef_lookup_model2.items()},
    "states": state_options,
    "titles": title_options,
    "occupation_groups": occupation_group_options,
    "specialized_occupations": specialized_occupation_options,
    "remotes": remote_options,
    "employments": employment_options,
    "educations": education_options,
    "skills": [
        "has_python",
        "has_sql",
        "has_machine_learning",
        "has_tableau",
        "has_power_bi",
        "has_aws",
        "has_excel",
        "has_sap"
    ]
}

payload_json = json.dumps(payload)

html = f"""
<div id="salary-widget" style="border:1px solid #ddd; border-radius:12px; padding:18px; max-width:900px; font-family:Arial, sans-serif;">
  <h3 style="margin-top:0;">Model 2 Salary Prediction Feature Explorer</h3>
  <p style="color:#555;">Select job characteristics and toggle skills to see how Model 2 features change predicted salary.</p>

  <div style="display:grid; grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr; gap:14px;">
    <label>State<br><select id="sw-state" style="width:100%; padding:6px;"></select></label>
    <label>Job Title Group<br><select id="sw-title" style="width:100%; padding:6px;"></select></label>
    <label>Occupation Group<br><select id="sw-occupation-group" style="width:100%; padding:6px;"></select></label>
    <label>Specialized Occupation<br><select id="sw-specialized-occupation" style="width:100%; padding:6px;"></select></label>
    <label>Remote Type<br><select id="sw-remote" style="width:100%; padding:6px;"></select></label>
    <label>Employment Type<br><select id="sw-employment" style="width:100%; padding:6px;"></select></label>
    <label>Education Level<br><select id="sw-education" style="width:100%; padding:6px;"></select></label>
    <label>Minimum Years Experience<br><input id="sw-years" type="number" value="0" min="0" step="1" style="width:100%; padding:6px;"></label>
  </div>

  <h4>Skills Mentioned in Posting</h4>
  <div id="sw-skills" style="display:grid; grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr; gap:8px;"></div>

  <div style="margin-top:18px; padding:14px; background:#f8f9fa; border-radius:10px;">
    <div style="font-size:13px; color:#666;">Predicted Salary</div>
    <div id="sw-prediction" style="font-size:34px; font-weight:bold;">$0</div>
    <div id="sw-factors" style="margin-top:12px; font-size:14px;"></div>
  </div>
</div>

<label>AI Related Job<br>
  <select id="sw-ai" style="width:100%; padding:6px;">
    <option value="0">No</option>
    <option value="1">Yes</option>
  </select>
</label>

<script>
const salaryModel = {payload_json};

function fixMojibake(s) {{
  return s.replace(/â‰¤/g, "≤").replace(/â€™/g, "'");
}}

function addOptions(selectId, values) {{
  const el = document.getElementById(selectId);
  values.forEach(v => {{
    const opt = document.createElement("option");
    opt.value = v;
    opt.textContent = fixMojibake(v);
    el.appendChild(opt);
  }});
}}

function prettySkill(s) {{
  const names = {{
    "has_python": "Python",
    "has_sql": "SQL",
    "has_machine_learning": "Machine Learning",
    "has_tableau": "Tableau",
    "has_power_bi": "Power BI",
    "has_aws": "AWS",
    "has_excel": "Excel",
    "has_sap": "SAP"
  }};
  return names[s] || s.replace("has_", "").replaceAll("_", " ");
}}

function money(x) {{
  return "$" + Math.round(x).toLocaleString();
}}

function updatePrediction() {{
  let pred = salaryModel.intercept;
  let factors = [];

  const state = document.getElementById("sw-state").value;
  const title = document.getElementById("sw-title").value;
  const occupationGroup = document.getElementById("sw-occupation-group").value;
  const specializedOccupation = document.getElementById("sw-specialized-occupation").value;
  const remote = document.getElementById("sw-remote").value;
  const education = document.getElementById("sw-education").value;
  const years = parseFloat(document.getElementById("sw-years").value || "0");
  const employment = document.getElementById("sw-employment").value;
  const isAI = parseFloat(document.getElementById("sw-ai").value || "0");

  const featureKeys = [
    "State: " + state,
    "Title: " + title,
    "Occupation Group: " + occupationGroup,
    "Specialized Occupation: " + specializedOccupation,
    "Remote: " + remote,
    "Education: " + education,
    "Employment: " + employment,
    "Is AI Related: " + isAI
  ];

  featureKeys.forEach(k => {{
    const c = salaryModel.coef[k] || 0;
    pred += c;
    if (Math.abs(c) > 1) factors.push([k, c]);
  }});

  const yearCoef = salaryModel.coef["Minimum Years Experience"] || 0;
  pred += yearCoef * years;
  if (Math.abs(yearCoef * years) > 1) factors.push(["Minimum Years Experience", yearCoef * years]);
  const aiCoef = salaryModel.coef["Is AI Related"] || 0;
pred += aiCoef * isAI;
if (Math.abs(aiCoef * isAI) > 1) factors.push(["Is AI Related", aiCoef * isAI]);

  salaryModel.skills.forEach(s => {{
    const box = document.getElementById("sw-" + s);
    if (box && box.checked) {{
      const key = "Skill: " + prettySkill(s);
      const c = salaryModel.coef[key] || 0;
      pred += c;
      if (Math.abs(c) > 1) factors.push([key, c]);
    }}
  }});

  document.getElementById("sw-prediction").textContent = money(pred);

  factors.sort((a,b) => Math.abs(b[1]) - Math.abs(a[1]));
  const topFactors = factors.slice(0,5).map(([k,c]) => {{
    const arrow = c >= 0 ? "↑" : "↓";
    const color = c >= 0 ? "#2ca02c" : "#d62728";
    return `<div style="color:${{color}};">${{arrow}} ${{k}} (${{money(c)}})</div>`;
  }}).join("");

  document.getElementById("sw-factors").innerHTML =
    "<strong>Top factors in this prediction</strong>" + topFactors;
}}

addOptions("sw-state", salaryModel.states);
addOptions("sw-title", salaryModel.titles);
addOptions("sw-occupation-group", salaryModel.occupation_groups);
addOptions("sw-specialized-occupation", salaryModel.specialized_occupations);
addOptions("sw-remote", salaryModel.remotes);
addOptions("sw-education", salaryModel.educations);
addOptions("sw-employment", salaryModel.employments);


const skillsDiv = document.getElementById("sw-skills");
salaryModel.skills.forEach(s => {{
  const label = document.createElement("label");
  label.innerHTML = `<input type="checkbox" id="sw-${{s}}"> ${{prettySkill(s)}}`;
  skillsDiv.appendChild(label);
}});

document.querySelectorAll("#salary-widget select, #salary-widget input").forEach(el => {{
  el.addEventListener("change", updatePrediction);
  el.addEventListener("input", updatePrediction);
}});

updatePrediction();
</script>
"""

display(HTML(html))
```


## Model 3: Random Forest (Built on Model 2 Features)

Model 3 reuses Model 2's training/test split (`train_model2` / `test_model2`) and copies Model 2's preprocessing exactly — the same `StringIndexer`/`OneHotEncoder` stages for job title, occupation group, specialized occupation, state, remote type, employment type, and education, plus the same numeric and skill columns. The only thing that changes is the final estimator: `LinearRegression` is replaced with `RandomForestRegressor`. This way, any difference in R², RMSE, or MAE reflects the choice of model (linear vs. tree ensemble), not a difference in input features.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

from pyspark.ml.regression import RandomForestRegressor

# Model 3 = Model 2's preprocessing, copied and renamed (_model3), with only the
# final estimator swapped: LinearRegression -> RandomForestRegressor.

title_indexer_model3 = StringIndexer(
    inputCol="title_grouped",
    outputCol="title_idx",
    handleInvalid="keep"
)
title_encoder_model3 = OneHotEncoder(
    inputCol="title_idx",
    outputCol="title_vec",
    dropLast=False
)

occupation_group_indexer_model3 = StringIndexer(
    inputCol="occupation_group_name",
    outputCol="occupation_group_idx",
    handleInvalid="keep"
)
occupation_group_encoder_model3 = OneHotEncoder(
    inputCol="occupation_group_idx",
    outputCol="occupation_group_vec",
    dropLast=False
)

specialized_occupation_indexer_model3 = StringIndexer(
    inputCol="specialized_occupation_name",
    outputCol="specialized_occupation_idx",
    handleInvalid="keep"
)
specialized_occupation_encoder_model3 = OneHotEncoder(
    inputCol="specialized_occupation_idx",
    outputCol="specialized_occupation_vec",
    dropLast=False
)

state_indexer_model3 = StringIndexer(
    inputCol="state_name",
    outputCol="state_idx",
    handleInvalid="keep"
)
state_encoder_model3 = OneHotEncoder(
    inputCol="state_idx",
    outputCol="state_vec",
    dropLast=False
)

remote_indexer_model3 = StringIndexer(
    inputCol="remote_type_name",
    outputCol="remote_idx",
    handleInvalid="keep"
)
remote_encoder_model3 = OneHotEncoder(
    inputCol="remote_idx",
    outputCol="remote_vec",
    dropLast=False
)

employment_indexer_model3 = StringIndexer(
    inputCol="employment_type_name",
    outputCol="employment_idx",
    handleInvalid="keep"
)
employment_encoder_model3 = OneHotEncoder(
    inputCol="employment_idx",
    outputCol="employment_vec",
    dropLast=False
)

education_indexer_model3 = StringIndexer(
    inputCol="education_level_clean",
    outputCol="education_idx",
    handleInvalid="keep"
)
education_encoder_model3 = OneHotEncoder(
    inputCol="education_idx",
    outputCol="education_vec",
    dropLast=False
)

assembler_model3 = VectorAssembler(
    inputCols=[
        "title_vec",
        "occupation_group_vec",
        "specialized_occupation_vec",
        "state_vec",
        "remote_vec",
        "employment_vec",
        "education_vec",
        "min_years_experience",
        "is_ai_related"
    ] + skill_cols_model2,
    outputCol="features"
)

# <-- this is the only conceptual change from Model 2 -->
rf_model3_estimator = RandomForestRegressor(
    featuresCol="features",
    labelCol="salary",
    numTrees=100,
    maxDepth=10,
    seed=42
)

pipeline_model3 = Pipeline(stages=[
    title_indexer_model3, title_encoder_model3,
    occupation_group_indexer_model3, occupation_group_encoder_model3,
    specialized_occupation_indexer_model3, specialized_occupation_encoder_model3,
    state_indexer_model3, state_encoder_model3,
    remote_indexer_model3, remote_encoder_model3,
    employment_indexer_model3, employment_encoder_model3,
    education_indexer_model3, education_encoder_model3,
    assembler_model3, rf_model3_estimator
])

model3 = pipeline_model3.fit(train_model2)

predictions_model3 = model3.transform(test_model2)

# Model 3 metrics
r2_model3 = RegressionEvaluator(
    labelCol="salary",
    predictionCol="prediction",
    metricName="r2"
).evaluate(predictions_model3)

rmse_model3 = RegressionEvaluator(
    labelCol="salary",
    predictionCol="prediction",
    metricName="rmse"
).evaluate(predictions_model3)

mae_model3 = RegressionEvaluator(
    labelCol="salary",
    predictionCol="prediction",
    metricName="mae"
).evaluate(predictions_model3)

rf_model3 = model3.stages[-1]

n_model3 = predictions_model3.count()
p_model3 = len(rf_model3.featureImportances)

adjusted_r2_model3 = (
    1 - ((1 - r2_model3) * (n_model3 - 1) / (n_model3 - p_model3 - 1))
    if n_model3 > p_model3 + 1
    else None
)

metrics_df_model3 = pd.DataFrame({
    "Metric": ["R²", "Adjusted R²", "RMSE", "MAE"],
    "Value": [
        round(r2_model3, 4),
        round(adjusted_r2_model3, 4) if adjusted_r2_model3 is not None else None,
        round(rmse_model3, 2),
        round(mae_model3, 2)
    ]
})

# print("\n=== Model 3: Random Forest Performance ===")
display(metrics_df_model3)

rmse_pct_of_median_model3 = rmse_model3 / salary_stats_model1["median_salary"] * 100
mae_pct_of_median_model3 = mae_model3 / salary_stats_model1["median_salary"] * 100
print(f"Model 3 RMSE is {rmse_pct_of_median_model3:.1f}% of median salary")
print(f"Model 3 MAE is {mae_pct_of_median_model3:.1f}% of median salary")
```


### Feature Importance

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

importances_model3 = rf_model3.featureImportances.toArray()

# Same label-extraction pattern as Model 2's coefficient table, just pointed at
# model3's fitted StringIndexer stages instead of model2's.
title_labels_model3 = list(model3.stages[0].labels) + ["title_unseen"]
occupation_group_labels_model3 = list(model3.stages[2].labels) + ["occupation_group_unseen"]
specialized_occupation_labels_model3 = list(model3.stages[4].labels) + ["specialized_occupation_unseen"]
state_labels_model3 = list(model3.stages[6].labels) + ["state_unseen"]
remote_labels_model3 = list(model3.stages[8].labels) + ["remote_unseen"]
employment_labels_model3 = list(model3.stages[10].labels) + ["employment_unseen"]
education_labels_model3 = list(model3.stages[12].labels) + ["education_unseen"]

feature_names_model3 = (
    ["Title: " + x for x in title_labels_model3] +
    ["Occupation Group: " + x for x in occupation_group_labels_model3] +
    ["Specialized Occupation: " + x for x in specialized_occupation_labels_model3] +
    ["State: " + x for x in state_labels_model3] +
    ["Remote: " + x for x in remote_labels_model3] +
    ["Employment: " + x for x in employment_labels_model3] +
    ["Education: " + x for x in education_labels_model3] +
    ["Minimum Years Experience"] +
    ["Is AI Related"] +
    ["Skill: " + x.replace("has_", "").replace("_", " ").title() for x in skill_cols_model2]
)

feature_names_model3 = feature_names_model3[:len(importances_model3)]

importance_df_model3 = pd.DataFrame({
    "Feature": feature_names_model3,
    "Importance": importances_model3[:len(feature_names_model3)]
})

importance_df_model3 = importance_df_model3[
    ~importance_df_model3["Feature"].str.contains("unseen", case=False, na=False)
].sort_values("Importance", ascending=False)

print("\n=== Model 3: Top 20 Features by Importance (Random Forest) ===")
display(importance_df_model3.head(20))
```

## Which Features Matter Most for Prediction? (Model 3)

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

top_importance_plot_model3 = importance_df_model3.head(20).sort_values("Importance").copy()

top_importance_plot_model3["label"] = top_importance_plot_model3["Importance"].apply(
    lambda x: f"{x:.3f}"
)

def shorten_feature_name_model3(x):
    if x.startswith("Specialized Occupation: "):
        title = x.replace("Specialized Occupation: ", "")
        if len(title) > 42:
            title = title[:42] + "..."
        x = "Spec. Occ.: " + title

    if x.startswith("Occupation Group: "):
        x = x.replace("Occupation Group: ", "Occ. Group: ")

    if len(x) > 70:
        x = x[:70] + "..."

    return x

top_importance_plot_model3["Feature_short"] = top_importance_plot_model3["Feature"].apply(shorten_feature_name_model3)

top_importance_plot_model3["Feature_display"] = top_importance_plot_model3.apply(
    lambda r: f"{r['Feature_short']}  ({r['label']})",
    axis=1
)

fig_importance_model3 = px.bar(
    top_importance_plot_model3,
    x="Importance",
    y="Feature_display",
    orientation="h",
    title="Top 20 Features by Importance (Model 3: Random Forest)",
    labels={
        "Importance": "Relative Importance",
        "Feature_display": ""
    },
    color="Importance",
    color_continuous_scale="Blues",
    hover_data={
        "Feature": True,
        "Importance": ":.4f",
        "Feature_short": False,
        "Feature_display": False,
        "label": False
    }
)

fig_importance_model3.update_layout(
    template="plotly_white",
    height=900,
    width=1150,
    margin=dict(l=520, r=80, t=90, b=70),
    showlegend=False,
    coloraxis_showscale=False,
    title=dict(x=0.02, xanchor="left"),
    xaxis=dict(
        title="Relative Importance",
        gridcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0.08)"
    ),
    yaxis=dict(
        title="",
        automargin=True
    )
)

fig_importance_model3.show()
```

Random Forest feature importance is measured differently from linear regression coefficients: a coefficient tells you the direction and dollar size of a feature's effect, while importance only tells you how much a feature reduced prediction error across all trees, regardless of direction. The two tables are not directly comparable line-by-line, but together they show whether the features that matter most for prediction (Model 3) line up with the features that push salary up or down (Model 2).

This pattern is visible in the results above: Title dominates Model 2's coefficient table (e.g., "data engineer analytics" adds over \$100,000), but no individual title appears in Model 3's top 20 importance list. This is not a contradiction -- it is a direct consequence of one-hot encoding. Title is split into 30+ separate columns, so its predictive contribution gets divided across all of them, while a single-column feature like Minimum Years Experience keeps its full contribution concentrated in one place. The apparent drop in Title's importance reflects how the feature is encoded, not a real loss of predictive value.



## Final Model Comparison: MLR vs. Random Forest

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

comparison_df_all = pd.DataFrame({
    "Model": ["Model 1: Baseline MLR", "Model 2: Improved MLR", "Model 3: Random Forest"],
    "R²": [
        round(r2_model1, 4),
        round(r2_model2, 4),
        round(r2_model3, 4)
    ],
    "Adjusted R²": [
        round(adjusted_r2_model1, 4) if adjusted_r2_model1 is not None else None,
        round(adjusted_r2_model2, 4) if adjusted_r2_model2 is not None else None,
        round(adjusted_r2_model3, 4) if adjusted_r2_model3 is not None else None
    ],
    "RMSE": [
        round(rmse_model1, 2),
        round(rmse_model2, 2),
        round(rmse_model3, 2)
    ],
    "MAE": [
        round(mae_model1, 2),
        round(mae_model2, 2),
        round(mae_model3, 2)
    ]
})

display(comparison_df_all)
```


This table lines up all three models on the same four metrics:
- **R² / Adjusted R²**: higher means more salary variance explained — rising from 0.33 (Model 1) to 0.37 (Model 2) to 0.50 (Model 3).
- **RMSE**: lower means more accurate dollar predictions; in percentage-of-median-salary terms, it drops from 32.1% (Model 1) to 31.1% (Model 2) to 27.9% (Model 3).
- **MAE**: also lower-is-better, dropping from 24.3% to 23.4% to 20.4% of median salary across the same three models.

Because Model 3 trains on the same engineered features as Model 2, any gap between them is attributable to the model itself (random forest vs. linear regression) rather than to differences in feature engineering. Across all three metrics, the largest single improvement comes from this model-type switch rather than from the feature changes introduced in Model 2.

For anyone using this analysis to inform real decisions -- whether benchmarking a salary offer, setting pay bands, or flagging underpriced postings -- the practical takeaway is twofold. First, a non-linear model captures meaningfully more of the salary structure in this market than a linear one, so Model 3 should be the default choice when prediction accuracy matters most. Second, the linear models remain useful precisely because they are interpretable: Model 2's coefficients can tell a hiring manager "this specific title is worth \$X more," a statement Model 3 cannot make as directly. In practice, the two approaches complement each other -- Random Forest for the most accurate estimate, Multiple Linear Regression for explaining why that estimate looks the way it does.
